categories.name: Historical Biographies
categories.description: Christians throughout history who glorified God through extraordinary works

people.name: Johann Sebastian Bach
people.title: Composer
people.bio: German composer and musician of the Baroque period, widely regarded as one of the greatest composers of all time. His works include the Brandenburg Concertos, the Mass in B minor, and hundreds of cantatas.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who inscribed 'Soli Deo Gloria' (To God Alone Be the Glory) on his manuscripts. He composed extensively for the church and viewed his musical gifts as given by God for His glory.

videos.title: Bach and the Bible - The Faith Behind the Music
videos.note: Search for documentaries on Bach's faith

people.name: George Frideric Handel
people.title: Composer
people.bio: German-born British Baroque composer famous for his operas, oratorios, and concertos. Best known for 'Messiah,' one of the most performed choral works in Western music.
people.faith: A committed Christian whose masterwork 'Messiah' sets to music the life of Jesus Christ from prophecy through resurrection. Upon completing the 'Hallelujah Chorus,' he reportedly said, 'I did think I did see all Heaven before me, and the great God Himself.'

people.name: Felix Mendelssohn
people.title: Composer
people.bio: German composer, pianist, and conductor of the early Romantic period. Known for his symphonies, concertos, and oratorios including 'Elijah.'
people.faith: Raised in a Jewish family that converted to Christianity, Mendelssohn was a devout Lutheran. His oratorio 'Elijah' and symphony 'Reformation' reflect his deep Christian faith. He also revived interest in Bach's sacred works.

sources.title: Felix Mendelssohn — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his Christian faith and sacred compositions

sources.title: Elijah (Mendelssohn) — Wikipedia
sources.note: His major biblical oratorio

people.name: Fanny Crosby
people.title: Hymn Writer
people.bio: American mission worker, poet, lyricist, and composer who wrote over 8,000 hymns and gospel songs. Blind from infancy, she became one of the most prolific hymnists in history.
people.faith: Despite losing her sight as an infant, Crosby devoted her life to glorifying God through music. She wrote beloved hymns including 'Blessed Assurance,' 'To God Be the Glory,' and 'All the Way My Savior Leads Me.'

sources.title: Fanny Crosby — Wikipedia

people.name: C.S. Lewis
people.title: Author & Apologist
people.bio: British writer and lay theologian, author of 'The Chronicles of Narnia,' 'Mere Christianity,' and 'The Screwtape Letters.' Fellow of Magdalen College, Oxford and later Professor at Cambridge.
people.faith: A former atheist who converted to Christianity in 1931, Lewis became one of the most influential Christian apologists of the 20th century. He described his conversion in 'Surprised by Joy' and defended the faith in works like 'Mere Christianity.'

people.name: J.R.R. Tolkien
people.title: Author & Philologist
people.bio: English writer, philologist, and academic, best known for 'The Hobbit' and 'The Lord of the Rings.' Professor of Anglo-Saxon at Oxford University.
people.faith: A devout Roman Catholic whose faith deeply influenced his literary works. He was instrumental in C.S. Lewis's conversion to Christianity. He described 'The Lord of the Rings' as 'a fundamentally religious and Catholic work.'

sources.title: J. R. R. Tolkien — Wikipedia

people.name: John Milton
people.title: Poet
people.bio: English poet and intellectual who served as a civil servant during the Commonwealth. Best known for the epic poem 'Paradise Lost,' considered one of the greatest works in the English language.
people.faith: A deeply devout Puritan Christian, Milton's magnum opus 'Paradise Lost' aimed to 'justify the ways of God to men.' His works are saturated with biblical themes and theological reflection.

sources.title: John Milton — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his Puritan faith and theological writings

sources.note: His epic Christian poem retelling the Fall of Man

people.name: Fyodor Dostoevsky
people.title: Novelist
people.bio: Russian novelist, philosopher, and journalist. His major works include 'Crime and Punishment,' 'The Brothers Karamazov,' and 'The Idiot.'
people.faith: After a period of doubt, Dostoevsky experienced a profound renewal of faith during his imprisonment in Siberia. His novels explore the depths of human sin and the transforming power of Christ's love. He wrote, 'If anyone proved to me that Christ was outside the truth... I would remain with Christ.'

sources.title: Fyodor Dostoevsky — Wikipedia

sources.note: By Dostoevsky. His final novel, deeply exploring Christian themes of faith, doubt, and redemption

people.name: G.K. Chesterton
people.title: Author & Apologist
people.bio: English writer, philosopher, and Christian apologist. Author of the Father Brown mysteries, 'Orthodoxy,' 'The Everlasting Man,' and over 80 books.
people.faith: Initially an agnostic, Chesterton converted first to Anglicanism and then to Roman Catholicism. His book 'Orthodoxy' describes his intellectual journey to faith. C.S. Lewis credited 'The Everlasting Man' as key to his own conversion.

sources.title: G. K. Chesterton — Wikipedia

people.name: John Bunyan
people.title: Author & Preacher
people.bio: English writer and Puritan preacher best known for 'The Pilgrim's Progress,' one of the most significant works of English literature and the most widely read Christian allegory ever written.
people.faith: A Baptist preacher who was imprisoned for 12 years for preaching without a license. During his imprisonment he wrote 'The Pilgrim's Progress,' an allegory of the Christian journey from sin to salvation that has never been out of print.

sources.title: John Bunyan — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the imprisoned preacher who wrote Pilgrim's Progress

sources.title: The Pilgrim's Progress — Wikipedia

people.name: Augustine of Hippo
people.title: Theologian & Author
people.bio: Early Christian theologian and philosopher from North Africa. Bishop of Hippo Regius and author of 'Confessions' and 'The City of God,' among the most influential works in Western Christianity.
people.faith: After years of dissolute living and exploring various philosophies, Augustine dramatically converted to Christianity, famously hearing a child's voice saying 'Take up and read.' His 'Confessions' is one of the first autobiographies in Western literature and a testament to God's grace.

sources.title: Augustine of Hippo — Wikipedia
sources.note: Comprehensive article on the most influential Church Father

sources.note: By Augustine. His autobiographical account of conversion, one of the most important works in Christian literature

sources.note: By Augustine. His monumental work on Christian philosophy of history

people.name: Leo Tolstoy
people.title: Novelist
people.bio: Russian writer regarded as one of the greatest authors of all time. Best known for 'War and Peace' and 'Anna Karenina.'
people.faith: After a spiritual crisis in midlife, Tolstoy underwent a profound Christian conversion. He devoted the latter part of his life to understanding and following the teachings of Jesus, writing 'A Confession' and 'The Kingdom of God Is Within You.'

sources.title: Leo Tolstoy — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his spiritual crisis and Christian conversion

sources.note: Tolstoy's account of his spiritual crisis and turn to Christ

sources.note: By Tolstoy. His treatise on Christian nonviolence that influenced Gandhi

people.name: George MacDonald
people.title: Author & Minister
people.bio: Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister. A pioneering figure in fantasy literature, writing works such as 'Phantastes,' 'The Princess and the Goblin,' and 'Lilith.'
people.faith: A Congregationalist minister whose theology of God's love permeated all his writings. C.S. Lewis called MacDonald his 'master' and credited MacDonald's 'Phantastes' with 'baptizing his imagination.'

sources.title: George MacDonald — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the minister and fantasy pioneer who influenced C.S. Lewis

sources.title: Phantastes
sources.note: By George MacDonald. The fantasy novel C.S. Lewis credited with 'baptizing his imagination'

people.name: Isaac Newton
people.title: Physicist & Mathematician
people.bio: English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation, developed calculus, and made groundbreaking contributions to optics.
people.faith: Newton wrote more about theology than science. He believed the elegant design of the solar system pointed to God and wrote, 'This most beautiful system of the sun, planets and comets, could only proceed from the counsel and dominion of an intelligent and powerful Being.'

sources.title: Isaac Newton — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his extensive theological writings

sources.note: Detailed article on Newton's Christian beliefs and theological works

people.name: Blaise Pascal
people.title: Mathematician, Physicist & Philosopher
people.bio: French mathematician, physicist, inventor, and philosopher. Made foundational contributions to probability theory, projective geometry, and the development of the mechanical calculator.
people.faith: After a mystical experience on November 23, 1654 (his 'Night of Fire'), Pascal devoted his life to defending Christianity. His 'Pensées' remains one of the most eloquent defenses of the Christian faith ever written. He is the author of 'Pascal's Wager.'

sources.title: Blaise Pascal — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his 'Night of Fire' conversion experience

sources.title: Pensées — Wikipedia
sources.note: His famous unfinished defense of the Christian faith

sources.note: His famous philosophical argument for belief in God

people.name: Michael Faraday
people.title: Physicist & Chemist
people.bio: English scientist who contributed to the study of electromagnetism and electrochemistry. His discoveries include electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism, and electrolysis.
people.faith: A devout member of the Sandemanian church (a small Christian sect), Faraday's faith was central to his life. He served as a deacon and elder and saw no conflict between science and his Christian beliefs, viewing the study of nature as the study of God's creation.

sources.title: Michael Faraday — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his Sandemanian faith

sources.title: Glasite — Wikipedia
sources.note: About the Sandemanian/Glasite church Faraday belonged to

people.name: Gregor Mendel
people.title: Geneticist & Friar
people.bio: Augustinian friar and scientist who discovered the fundamental laws of inheritance through his work with pea plants. Known as the 'Father of Genetics.'
people.faith: An Augustinian friar who served as abbot of his monastery, Mendel's scientific work was conducted within the context of his religious life. He saw no contradiction between his faith and his scientific research.

sources.title: Gregor Mendel — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the Augustinian friar who founded genetics

people.name: George Washington Carver
people.title: Agricultural Scientist & Inventor
people.bio: American agricultural scientist and inventor who developed hundreds of products using peanuts, sweet potatoes, and soybeans. Born into slavery, he became one of the most prominent Black scientists of his era.
people.faith: A devout Christian who attributed all his discoveries to God. He famously said, 'I love to think of nature as an unlimited broadcasting station, through which God speaks to us every hour, if we will only tune in.' He taught a weekly Bible class for over 30 years.

sources.title: George Washington Carver — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his deep Christian faith and Bible teaching

sources.title: George Washington Carver: A Man of Faith and Science
sources.note: Documentaries about his faith-driven scientific discoveries

people.name: Louis Pasteur
people.title: Chemist & Microbiologist
people.bio: French chemist and microbiologist renowned for his discoveries of the principles of vaccination, microbial fermentation, and pasteurization. He is known as the 'Father of Microbiology.'
people.faith: A devout Catholic who maintained his faith throughout his scientific career. He stated, 'The more I study nature, the more I stand amazed at the work of the Creator.'

sources.title: Louis Pasteur — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his Catholic faith

sources.title: Religious views of Louis Pasteur — Wikipedia
sources.note: Discussion of Pasteur's Catholic beliefs

people.name: Johannes Kepler
people.title: Astronomer & Mathematician
people.bio: German astronomer, mathematician, and astrologer who discovered the three laws of planetary motion, foundational to Isaac Newton's theory of universal gravitation.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who saw his scientific work as a form of worship. He wrote, 'I was merely thinking God's thoughts after Him. Since we astronomers are priests of the highest God in regard to the book of nature, it benefits us to be thoughtful.'

sources.title: Johannes Kepler — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his Lutheran faith and view of science as worship

people.name: Robert Boyle
people.title: Chemist & Physicist
people.bio: Anglo-Irish natural philosopher, chemist, physicist, and inventor, regarded as the first modern chemist. Best known for Boyle's Law relating gas pressure and volume.
people.faith: A deeply devout Christian who wrote extensively on theology and funded Bible translations. He established the Boyle Lectures for defending Christianity against atheism and viewed science as a way to understand God's creation.

sources.title: Robert Boyle — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his extensive theological writings and Bible translations

sources.title: Boyle Lectures — Wikipedia
sources.note: The lecture series Boyle established to defend Christianity

people.name: James Clerk Maxwell
people.title: Physicist & Mathematician
people.bio: Scottish physicist responsible for the classical theory of electromagnetic radiation, unifying electricity, magnetism, and light. Einstein called Maxwell's work 'the most profound and the most fruitful that physics has experienced since the time of Newton.'
people.faith: A deeply committed evangelical Presbyterian who prayed regularly and saw his physics as revealing God's glory. He wrote, 'I think that the results which each man arrives at in his attempts to harmonize his science with his Christianity ought not to be regarded as having any significance except to the man himself.'

sources.title: James Clerk Maxwell — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his evangelical Presbyterian faith

people.name: Francis Collins
people.title: Geneticist
people.bio: American physician-geneticist who led the Human Genome Project and served as director of the National Institutes of Health (NIH) from 2009 to 2021.
people.faith: A former atheist who converted to Christianity after reading C.S. Lewis's 'Mere Christianity.' He wrote 'The Language of God: A Scientist Presents Evidence for Belief' and founded BioLogos to explore the harmony between science and faith.

videos.title: Francis Collins: Why I'm a Christian
videos.note: Search for Collins's numerous faith interviews and talks

people.name: Leonhard Euler
people.title: Mathematician & Physicist
people.bio: Swiss mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who made foundational contributions to numerous areas of mathematics. He is considered one of the greatest mathematicians of all time.
people.faith: A devout Calvinist who wrote a defense of Christianity called 'Letters to a German Princess,' which included arguments for the existence of God. He held daily family prayers and composed hymns.

sources.title: Leonhard Euler — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his Calvinist faith and defense of Christianity

people.name: Bernhard Riemann
people.title: Mathematician
people.bio: German mathematician who made lasting contributions to analysis, number theory, and differential geometry, including the Riemann hypothesis and Riemannian geometry (which provided the mathematical foundation for Einstein's general relativity).
people.faith: The son of a Lutheran pastor, Riemann was a devout Christian throughout his life. He originally planned to study theology before pursuing mathematics.

sources.title: Bernhard Riemann — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his pastoral family background and original intent to study theology

people.name: Georg Cantor
people.title: Mathematician
people.bio: German mathematician who created set theory and established the importance of one-to-one correspondence between sets, defining infinite and well-ordered sets.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who believed his theory of transfinite numbers was communicated to him by God. He saw the absolute infinite as an expression of the nature of God.

sources.title: Georg Cantor — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his belief that God communicated set theory to him

people.name: Michelangelo Buonarroti
people.title: Artist, Sculptor & Architect
people.bio: Italian sculptor, painter, architect, and poet of the High Renaissance. Created the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel, the statue of David, and the Pietà.
people.faith: A devout Catholic whose greatest works depicted biblical scenes and Christian themes. His Sistine Chapel ceiling telling the story of Genesis and his Pietà depicting Mary holding the body of Christ are among the most recognizable Christian artworks ever created.

sources.title: Michelangelo — Wikipedia
sources.note: Comprehensive biography including his faith-driven art

sources.title: Sistine Chapel ceiling — Wikipedia
sources.note: His masterwork depicting the Genesis narrative

people.name: Rembrandt van Rijn
people.title: Painter & Printmaker
people.bio: Dutch Golden Age painter and printmaker, generally considered one of the greatest visual artists in history. Known for biblical paintings, self-portraits, and masterful use of light and shadow.
people.faith: A deeply devout Protestant Christian, Rembrandt painted over 300 biblical scenes, more than any other major Western artist. His 'The Return of the Prodigal Son' is considered one of the most powerful visual depictions of God's grace.

sources.title: Rembrandt — Wikipedia

people.name: Albrecht Dürer
people.title: Artist & Printmaker
people.bio: German painter, printmaker, and theorist of the German Renaissance. Known for his woodcuts, engravings, and paintings including 'Praying Hands.'
people.faith: A devout Christian who embraced the Protestant Reformation. His famous 'Praying Hands' drawing and numerous biblical engravings reflect his deep faith. He admired Martin Luther and incorporated Reformation theology into his later works.

sources.title: Albrecht Dürer — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his embrace of the Reformation

sources.title: Praying Hands (Dürer) — Wikipedia
sources.note: His iconic drawing of hands in prayer

people.name: Antoni Gaudí
people.title: Architect
people.bio: Spanish architect and the best-known practitioner of Catalan Modernism. His masterwork, the Sagrada Família basilica in Barcelona, remains under construction more than a century after his death.
people.faith: A devout Catholic whose later years were entirely dedicated to building the Sagrada Família as an offering to God. He lived ascetically and attended Mass daily. He is currently a candidate for canonization in the Catholic Church.

sources.title: Antoni Gaudí — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography including his devout faith and canonization cause

sources.title: Sagrada Família — Wikipedia
sources.note: His life's masterwork dedicated to the Holy Family

people.name: Martin Luther
people.title: Reformer & Theologian
people.bio: German professor of theology, priest, and seminal figure in the Protestant Reformation. His Ninety-five Theses challenged the Catholic Church's practices and launched the Reformation.
people.faith: Luther's rediscovery of justification by faith alone (sola fide) through Scripture alone (sola scriptura) transformed Christianity. His translation of the Bible into German made Scripture accessible to ordinary people.

sources.note: Comprehensive biography of the father of the Reformation

people.name: John Calvin
people.title: Reformer & Theologian
people.bio: French theologian, pastor, and reformer who was a principal figure in the development of the system of Christian theology later called Calvinism. His 'Institutes of the Christian Religion' is a foundational work of Protestant theology.
people.faith: Calvin's theology emphasized the sovereignty of God, the authority of Scripture, and salvation by grace alone through faith alone. His work in Geneva established a model for Reformed churches worldwide.

sources.title: John Calvin — Wikipedia
sources.note: Comprehensive biography of the Reformed theologian

sources.title: Institutes of the Christian Religion — Wikipedia
sources.note: His foundational work of Protestant theology

people.name: Jonathan Edwards
people.title: Theologian & Preacher
people.bio: American revivalist preacher, philosopher, and Congregationalist Protestant theologian. Key figure in the First Great Awakening and third president of Princeton University.
people.faith: Edwards is widely regarded as America's greatest theologian. His sermon 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' is one of the most famous sermons in American history. His theological works on the nature of true virtue and religious affections remain deeply influential.

people.name: Charles Haddon Spurgeon
people.title: Preacher & Author
people.bio: English Particular Baptist preacher known as the 'Prince of Preachers.' Pastor of the Metropolitan Tabernacle in London, he frequently preached to audiences of more than 10,000 people.
people.faith: Spurgeon was converted at age 15 and began preaching immediately. He published thousands of sermons and authored numerous books. He founded a pastors' college, orphanages, and other charitable organizations, all flowing from his deep love for Christ.

sources.title: Charles Spurgeon — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the 'Prince of Preachers'

sources.title: Spurgeon's Sermons Archive
sources.note: Archive of Spurgeon's thousands of published sermons

people.name: John Wesley
people.title: Theologian & Evangelist
people.bio: English cleric, theologian, and evangelist who co-founded the Methodist movement. His open-air preaching brought the gospel to millions across England.
people.faith: After his famous 'Aldersgate experience' in 1738 where his heart was 'strangely warmed,' Wesley dedicated his life to evangelism. He traveled 250,000 miles on horseback, preached over 40,000 sermons, and sparked a revival that transformed England.

sources.title: John Wesley — Wikipedia
sources.note: Comprehensive biography of the founder of Methodism

sources.title: Aldersgate experience — Wikipedia
sources.note: Wesley's famous heart-warming conversion experience

people.name: George Whitefield
people.title: Evangelist & Preacher
people.bio: Anglican cleric and evangelist who was one of the founders of Methodism and the evangelical movement. He preached to enormous crowds on both sides of the Atlantic and was a key figure in the Great Awakening.
people.faith: Whitefield was one of the most powerful preachers in Christian history, known for his dramatic delivery and deep conviction. Benjamin Franklin estimated he could be heard by 30,000 people at once. He preached over 18,000 sermons in his lifetime.

sources.title: George Whitefield — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the Great Awakening evangelist

people.name: William Wilberforce
people.title: Politician & Reformer
people.bio: British politician and philanthropist who led the movement to abolish the slave trade in the British Empire. Member of Parliament for 45 years.
people.faith: After his evangelical conversion in 1785, Wilberforce devoted his life to two 'great objects': the abolition of the slave trade and the reformation of manners (moral reform). His Christian faith was the driving force behind his tireless campaign against slavery.

sources.title: William Wilberforce — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the abolitionist driven by faith

sources.note: Biographical film about Wilberforce's fight to end slavery

sources.title: Amazing Grace: William Wilberforce and the Heroic Campaign to End Slavery

people.name: Dietrich Bonhoeffer
people.title: Theologian & Martyr
people.bio: German Lutheran pastor, theologian, and anti-Nazi dissident. Author of 'The Cost of Discipleship' and 'Life Together.' Executed by the Nazis at Flossenbürg concentration camp.
people.faith: Bonhoeffer's theology of 'costly grace' challenged cheap Christianity. He helped found the Confessing Church in opposition to Nazi influence and participated in a plot to assassinate Hitler. His final words were, 'This is the end — for me, the beginning of life.'

sources.title: Dietrich Bonhoeffer — Wikipedia
sources.note: Biography of the theologian-martyr executed by the Nazis

people.name: Billy Graham
people.title: Evangelist
people.bio: American evangelist and ordained Southern Baptist minister whose broadcasts and world tours spanning over six decades made him one of the most influential Christian leaders of the 20th century. He preached to live audiences of nearly 215 million people in more than 185 countries.
people.faith: Known as 'America's Pastor,' Graham preached a simple message of salvation through Jesus Christ. He served as spiritual advisor to multiple U.S. Presidents and founded the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association. His integrity and humility set a standard for public Christian ministry.

people.name: Jim Elliot
people.title: Missionary & Martyr
people.bio: American Christian missionary who was killed along with four companions during Operation Auca, an attempt to evangelize the Huaorani people of Ecuador. He was 28 years old. His death galvanized a generation of missionaries.
people.faith: Elliot famously wrote in his journal, 'He is no fool who gives what he cannot keep to gain what he cannot lose.' His wife Elisabeth Elliot later returned to minister to the very tribe that killed him, and many Huaorani eventually became Christians.

people.name: Hudson Taylor
people.title: Missionary to China
people.bio: British Protestant Christian missionary to China and founder of the China Inland Mission (now OMF International). He spent 51 years in China and is regarded as one of the most important missionaries of all time.
people.faith: Taylor pioneered a new approach to missions by adopting Chinese dress and customs, living by faith without soliciting funds, and opening inland China to the gospel. The CIM he founded became the largest mission agency in the world, eventually bringing over 800 missionaries to China.

people.name: Eric Liddell
people.title: Olympic Runner & Missionary
people.bio: Scottish Olympic gold medalist (400m, 1924 Paris Olympics) who refused to run his best event (100m) because the heat was on a Sunday. He later served as a missionary in China, where he died in a Japanese internment camp during World War II.
people.faith: Liddell famously said, 'God made me fast. And when I run, I feel His pleasure.' After his Olympic triumph, he devoted his life to missionary work in China. His story was immortalized in the Academy Award-winning film 'Chariots of Fire.'

people.name: William Carey
people.title: Missionary to India
people.bio: English Baptist minister, missionary, and translator known as the 'Father of Modern Missions.' He founded the Serampore College and Serampore University (the first degree-granting university in India) and translated the Bible into numerous Indian languages.
people.faith: Carey's famous motto was 'Expect great things from God; attempt great things for God.' He labored for decades in India, translating the Bible into Bengali, Sanskrit, and many other languages, establishing schools, and fighting social evils like sati (widow burning).

people.name: Adoniram Judson
people.title: Missionary to Burma
people.bio: American Congregationalist and later Baptist missionary who served in Burma (Myanmar) for nearly 40 years. He was the first American foreign missionary sent overseas and translated the entire Bible into Burmese.
people.faith: Judson endured imprisonment, torture, the deaths of two wives and several children, and decades of hardship for the sake of the gospel. He completed the Burmese Bible translation and established churches that endure to this day.

people.name: David Livingstone
people.title: Missionary & Explorer
people.bio: Scottish Congregationalist pioneer Christian missionary, doctor, and explorer in Africa. He was the first European to see Victoria Falls and made extensive explorations of central Africa while seeking to end the slave trade.
people.faith: Livingstone believed that Christianity and commerce together could end the African slave trade. He spent over 30 years in Africa, often alone, driven by his faith and desire to bring the gospel to unreached peoples. His body was buried in Westminster Abbey.

people.name: George Müller
people.title: Evangelist & Orphanage Director
people.bio: Prussian-born evangelist and director of the Ashley Down orphanage in Bristol, England. He cared for over 10,000 orphans during his lifetime and established 117 schools offering Christian education to over 120,000 children.
people.faith: Müller is renowned for his radical faith in God's provision. He never asked for money or went into debt, instead relying entirely on prayer. He recorded over 50,000 specific answers to prayer in his journals. He also distributed over 2 million Bibles and tracts.

people.name: Corrie ten Boom
people.title: Holocaust Survivor & Evangelist
people.bio: Dutch Christian watchmaker who, along with her family, helped many Jews escape the Nazi Holocaust by hiding them in her home. She was eventually imprisoned in the Ravensbrück concentration camp, where her sister Betsie died.
people.faith: After surviving the concentration camp, ten Boom traveled the world as a speaker, sharing her message of forgiveness and God's love. Her autobiography 'The Hiding Place' tells the story of her family's courageous faith. She famously forgave a former Ravensbrück guard face-to-face.

people.name: John MacArthur
people.title: Pastor & Author
people.bio: American pastor-teacher who served as pastor of Grace Community Church in Sun Valley, California, for over 55 years. He was also president of The Master's University and Seminary, a prolific author of over 150 books, and the voice behind the 'Grace to You' radio broadcast.
people.faith: MacArthur was one of the most influential Bible expositors of his generation. His verse-by-verse preaching style and commitment to biblical inerrancy shaped Reformed evangelical Christianity. His MacArthur Study Bible has sold over 2 million copies.

people.name: R.C. Sproul
people.title: Theologian & Pastor
people.bio: American Reformed theologian, pastor, and founder of Ligonier Ministries. He authored over 100 books and was known for making complex theology accessible to ordinary Christians. He served as co-pastor at Saint Andrew's Chapel in Sanford, Florida.
people.faith: Sproul dedicated his life to teaching the holiness of God and Reformed theology. His book 'The Holiness of God' is considered a modern classic. He was instrumental in drafting the Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy (1978).

people.name: D.L. Moody
people.title: Evangelist & Educator
people.bio: American evangelist and publisher connected with Keswick and Higher Life movements. He founded the Moody Church, Northfield Schools, and the Moody Bible Institute in Chicago. He is estimated to have preached to over 100 million people.
people.faith: Moody was converted at age 18 when his Sunday school teacher visited him at work. He became one of the greatest evangelists of the 19th century, working alongside hymn writer Ira Sankey. His plain-spoken gospel preaching drew massive crowds on both sides of the Atlantic.

people.name: Amy Carmichael
people.title: Missionary to India
people.bio: Irish Christian missionary who served in India for 55 years without furlough. She founded the Dohnavur Fellowship and devoted her life to rescuing children from temple prostitution. She wrote 35 books about her missionary work.
people.faith: Carmichael gave her entire life to serving the most vulnerable in India. She rescued hundreds of children from abuse and exploitation, establishing a community that still operates today. Her writings on the cost of discipleship continue to inspire missionaries worldwide.

people.name: Jim Cymbala
people.title: Pastor & Author
people.bio: American pastor of the Brooklyn Tabernacle in New York City. Under his leadership, the church grew from fewer than 20 members to over 10,000. The Brooklyn Tabernacle Choir, directed by his wife Carol, has won multiple Grammy Awards.
people.faith: Cymbala transformed a dying inner-city church through prayer. His Tuesday night prayer meetings became legendary, drawing thousands. His book 'Fresh Wind, Fresh Fire' recounts how God revived the Brooklyn Tabernacle through fervent prayer.

people.name: Francis Schaeffer
people.title: Philosopher, Theologian & Author
people.bio: American evangelical theologian, philosopher, and Presbyterian pastor. He founded the L'Abri Fellowship community in Switzerland, which became a haven for seekers and intellectuals exploring Christian faith. He authored influential works including 'The God Who Is There' and 'How Should We Then Live?'
people.faith: Schaeffer engaged the intellectual culture of the 20th century with a rigorous Christian worldview. He argued that Christianity provides the only adequate basis for human meaning, morality, and knowledge. His work influenced a generation of evangelical thinkers and leaders.

people.name: John Piper
people.title: Pastor & Theologian
people.bio: American Reformed Baptist pastor, author, and theologian. He served as pastor of Bethlehem Baptist Church in Minneapolis for 33 years and founded Desiring God Ministries. He has authored over 50 books including 'Desiring God' and 'Don't Waste Your Life.'
people.faith: Piper is known for his concept of 'Christian Hedonism' — the idea that 'God is most glorified in us when we are most satisfied in Him.' His expository preaching, passionate delivery, and emphasis on God's sovereignty have shaped a generation of Reformed evangelicals.

people.name: A.W. Tozer
people.title: Pastor & Author
people.bio: American Christian pastor, author, magazine editor, and spiritual mentor. He served as pastor for 44 years, primarily at Southside Alliance Church in Chicago. Despite having no formal education, he received honorary doctorates from Wheaton and Houghton colleges.
people.faith: Tozer's devotional writings emphasize the pursuit of God's presence above all else. His books 'The Pursuit of God' and 'The Knowledge of the Holy' are considered spiritual classics. He was known as a 'prophet' who called the church to deeper intimacy with God.

people.name: Lottie Moon
people.title: Missionary to China
people.bio: American Southern Baptist missionary who served in China for nearly 40 years. She spent her life evangelizing and advocating for mission support. The annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering for international missions, named in her honor, has raised billions of dollars.
people.faith: Moon gave everything for the gospel in China, eventually dying of starvation because she had given all her food to starving Chinese during a famine. Her sacrificial example inspired the Southern Baptist Convention's largest missions offering, still collected annually.

people.name: Watchman Nee
people.title: Church Leader & Author
people.bio: Chinese church leader and Christian author who founded the Local Church movement in China. He authored influential works including 'The Normal Christian Life' and 'Sit, Walk, Stand.' He was imprisoned by the Communist government in 1952 and died in prison after 20 years.
people.faith: Nee's emphasis on the inner spiritual life and the practical experience of Christ's indwelling has influenced millions of Christians worldwide. He endured two decades of imprisonment for his faith without renouncing Christ.

people.name: Charles Finney
people.title: Evangelist & Revivalist
people.bio: American Presbyterian minister, revivalist leader, and president of Oberlin College. He was a central figure in the Second Great Awakening and is estimated to have led over 500,000 people to Christ.
people.faith: Finney's dramatic conversion experience in 1821 transformed him from a lawyer to a fiery evangelist. His revival meetings featured new methods including the 'anxious bench' and extended meetings. He was also a strong advocate for abolition and women's education.

people.name: William Booth
people.title: Evangelist & Social Reformer
people.bio: English Methodist preacher who co-founded The Salvation Army with his wife Catherine in 1865. The organization combined evangelism with social services for the poor and has grown into a worldwide movement operating in over 130 countries.
people.faith: Booth's passion was bringing the gospel to the poorest and most neglected members of society. He famously said, 'While women weep, as they do now, I'll fight; while little children go hungry, as they do now, I'll fight.' The Salvation Army's model of practical Christianity has served millions.

people.name: Gladys Aylward
people.title: Missionary to China
people.bio: English missionary to China who served from 1930 onward. During the Japanese invasion, she led over 100 orphan children on a harrowing journey across the mountains to safety. Her story was told in the film 'The Inn of the Sixth Happiness.'
people.faith: Rejected by the China Inland Mission for lacking education, Aylward saved her own money to travel to China by the Trans-Siberian Railway. She adopted Chinese citizenship, ran an inn for muleteers where she told Bible stories, and became known as 'Ai-weh-deh' (the Virtuous One).
